introduction: A neurologic disorder characterized by increased muscle tone and spasm caused by tetanospasmin, a powerful protein toxin elaborated by Clostridium tetani. C. tetani is ubiquitous and affects all ages and sexes, especially in low socioeconomic settings. The bacteria are found in the soil, inanimate environment, animal faeces, and occasionally in human faeces. Portals of entry include the umbilical stump, female genital mutilation, male circumcision, abortion sites, penetrative wounds (e.g., nail puncture or intramuscular injection), head injury, scalp wounds, traditional scarification, trado-medical incisions, post-operative surgical sites, and chronic otitis media.

features: Stiffness or pain in the neck, shoulder, and back muscles
Rigid abdomen and stiff proximal limb muscles
Intermittent reflex spasms in response to stimuli (e.g. noise, touch)
Increased tone: spasms are restricted to the muscles near the wound.
Follows head injury or ear infection.
Dysfunction of one or more cranial nerves, often the 7th nerve

investigations: Wound swab for microscopy, culture, and sensitivity
Cerebrospinal fluid for biochemistry; microscopy, culture, and sensitivity
Urinalysis; urine microscopy, culture, and sensitivity

goals: Monitor the patient's condition and provide support until recovery.
non_drug: Admit patient to a dark and quiet room or intensive care unit where available.
Cleanse and thoroughly debride the wound.
Provide intubation or tracheostomy for hypoventilation.
Monitor bowel, bladder and renal function.
drug: Metronidazole - Adult: 500 mg intravenously, every 6 hours for 10 days
Benzylpenicillin (Penicillin G) - Adult: 0.6 - 2.4 g daily by slow intravenous injection or infusion in 2 - 4 divided doses; higher doses in severe infections
Human tetanus immune globulin (TIG) - Adult: TIG 500 units by IM injection or intravenously (IV)—depending on the available preparation
Diazepam - Adult: 20 mg intravenously slowly stat and titrate up to 250 mg/day in infusion
Phenobarbital - Adult: 10 mg/kg intravenously at a rate of not more than 100 mg/minute, up to maximum total dose of 1 g
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Diazepam is adsorbed from plastics of infusion bags and giving sets; causes drowsiness and light headedness; hypotension.
Phenobarbital: caution in renal and hepatic impairment; can cause paradoxical excitement, restlessness, and confusion in the elderly; hyperkinesia in children.
supportive_measures: Treat autonomic dysfunction with vasopressors, chronotropic agents if necessary
Hydration: To control insensitive and other fluid losses
Enteral or parenteral nutrition as determined by clinical situation

complications: Autonomic dysfunction - Labile or sustained hypertension, Tachycardia, Dysarrhythmias, Hyperpyrexia, Profuse sweating, Peripheral vasoconstriction, Cardiac arrest
prevention: Active immunization of all partially or un-immunized adults, those recovering from tetanus, all pregnant women, infants and un-immunized (missed) children.
